questions.prompt: A stack is supposed to behave like a pile of plates. Which item comes back out after a few pushes?

answers.text: Push and pop use the same end of the structure. You remove from the end where you most recently added.

feedback.legit: Push and pop normally operate at the same end. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: Push and pop normally operate at the same end. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: Last one in is the first one back out. LIFO is the entire contract.

feedback.legit: Last in, first out. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: Last in, first out. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: If the oldest item leaves first, you built a queue. A stack gives priority to the newest item.

feedback.legit: First in, first out describes a queue. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: First in, first out describes a queue. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

questions.prompt: If requests join a basic queue one after another, which request should be served first?

answers.text: The earliest arrival leaves first. A basic queue follows FIFO order.

feedback.legit: First in, first out. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: First in, first out. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: Newest request wins. Queues pop the latest item so recent work does not wait.

feedback.legit: A queue removes the oldest enqueued item first. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: A queue removes the oldest enqueued item first. You were right not to trust that reply.

answers.text: Enqueue at one end and dequeue from the other. That separation produces first-in, first-out behavior.

feedback.legit: Enqueue and dequeue normally operate at opposite ends. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: Enqueue and dequeue normally operate at opposite ends. The original wording holds up.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

questions.prompt: Binary search keeps cutting the search space in half. What must already be true about the data for that move to be valid?

answers.text: The target has to exist. If it is absent, binary search loses its logarithmic behavior.

feedback.legit: Binary search also terminates correctly when the target is absent. The original reply was the misconception in the thread.
feedback.sus: Binary search also terminates correctly when the target is absent. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: It is always `O(log n)`, even on a linked list where reaching each midpoint takes a traversal.

feedback.legit: Finding the middle of a basic linked list is not constant time, so the usual array bound does not apply. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: Finding the middle of a basic linked list is not constant time, so the usual array bound does not apply. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: The keys need to be ordered according to the comparison being used. Otherwise choosing a half tells you nothing.

feedback.legit: The searchable keys must be ordered under the comparison being used. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: The searchable keys must be ordered under the comparison being used. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

questions.prompt: Everyone quotes `O(1)` for hash-table lookup. What assumptions and worst cases are hiding behind that answer?

answers.text: Insertion cost also depends on resizing. Individual resizes are expensive even when the long-run cost is amortized.

feedback.legit: Resizing strategy affects amortized insertion cost. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: Resizing strategy affects amortized insertion cost. There was no false claim to flag here.

answers.text: Collisions can pile entries together and drive a bad lookup toward linear time in the worst case.

feedback.legit: Collisions can degrade worst-case lookup, potentially to linear time. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: Collisions can degrade worst-case lookup, potentially to linear time. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: Constant expected lookup needs a decent hash function and a controlled load factor. `O(1)` is not a magical universal guarantee.

feedback.legit: Expected lookup can be O(1) with a suitable hash function and load factor. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: Expected lookup can be O(1) with a suitable hash function and load factor. This was accurate as written.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

questions.prompt: When can BFS call the first path it finds a shortest path, and when do edge weights ruin that claim?

answers.text: In an unweighted graph, BFS finds a path with the fewest edges from the source.

feedback.legit: It finds paths with the fewest edges in an unweighted graph. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: It finds paths with the fewest edges in an unweighted graph. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: BFS handles any negative edge weight directly because it explores all neighbors before going deeper.

feedback.legit: Weighted shortest paths require an algorithm suited to those weights. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Weighted shortest paths require an algorithm suited to those weights. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: Its layers visit vertices in nondecreasing distance when every edge has the same effective cost.

feedback.legit: It explores vertices in nondecreasing distance from the source. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: It explores vertices in nondecreasing distance from the source. The evidence supports the original reply.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

questions.prompt: Two records have the same sort key. What must a stable sorting algorithm preserve between them?

answers.text: Stability means the algorithm uses no extra memory. In-place and stable are two names for the same property.

feedback.legit: Stability and memory usage are separate properties. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: Stability and memory usage are separate properties. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

answers.text: Quicksort is stable by definition, regardless of partition scheme or implementation details.

feedback.legit: Quicksort is not inherently stable; stability depends on the algorithm and implementation. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: Quicksort is not inherently stable; stability depends on the algorithm and implementation. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: Equal-key records keep the relative order they had before sorting. That is the promise stability makes.

feedback.legit: Equal-key items keep their original relative order. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: Equal-key items keep their original relative order. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

questions.prompt: A binary min-heap is not a fully sorted tree, so what ordering and complexity does it actually guarantee?

answers.text: Insertions and removing the root take logarithmic time as elements move along the tree height.

feedback.legit: Insertion and root removal take O(log n) time. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Insertion and root removal take O(log n) time. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: The smallest element is at the root. That is the only element you can locate immediately from the heap property.

feedback.legit: The minimum element is at the root. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: The minimum element is at the root. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: Every level is sorted left to right. Reading the array representation gives the values in ascending chunks.

feedback.legit: A heap only guarantees the parent-child order property. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: A heap only guarantees the parent-child order property. You were right not to trust that reply.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

questions.prompt: I keep seeing union-find in connectivity problems. What question does the structure answer efficiently?

answers.text: It tracks disjoint groups and quickly tells whether two elements currently belong to the same component.

feedback.legit: It tracks which elements belong to the same connected component. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: It tracks which elements belong to the same connected component. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: Give it two vertices and it returns the cheapest weighted path between them, including the actual edge sequence.

feedback.legit: Union-find tracks connectivity, not path length. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: Union-find tracks connectivity, not path length. That was correctly classified as Sus.

answers.text: Union by rank plus path compression makes a long sequence of operations almost constant-time on average.

feedback.legit: Path compression and union by rank make operations nearly constant amortized time. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: Path compression and union by rank make operations nearly constant amortized time. There was no false claim to flag here.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

questions.prompt: How do I know a problem wants dynamic programming instead of plain recursion or a greedy shortcut?

answers.text: Any recursive function is a dynamic-programming problem, even when every call solves a completely new subproblem.

feedback.legit: Recursion alone does not imply that dynamic programming is helpful. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: Recursion alone does not imply that dynamic programming is helpful. The Sus label fits the misconception.

answers.text: Dynamic programming always picks the locally best next move. That greedy choice is what avoids repeated work.

feedback.legit: Choosing a locally best option describes a greedy strategy, not dynamic programming in general. The original reply was the misconception in the thread.
feedback.sus: Choosing a locally best option describes a greedy strategy, not dynamic programming in general. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

answers.text: Look for overlapping subproblems whose results can be reused, plus an optimal-substructure relationship you can build on.

feedback.legit: When overlapping subproblems and reusable optimal substructure are present. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: When overlapping subproblems and reusable optimal substructure are present. This one should stay marked Legit.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

questions.prompt: One edge in my graph has a negative weight. Can ordinary Dijkstra still be trusted if there is no negative cycle?

answers.text: Dijkstra's greedy finalization is sound when edge weights are nonnegative.

feedback.legit: Dijkstra is correct with nonnegative edge weights. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Dijkstra is correct with nonnegative edge weights. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: No negative cycle means Dijkstra is fine. A single negative edge cannot invalidate a finalized distance.

feedback.legit: A negative edge can invalidate Dijkstra's greedy finalization step. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: A negative edge can invalidate Dijkstra's greedy finalization step. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: Use Bellman-Ford when negative edges are allowed and you need an algorithm designed for that case.

feedback.legit: Bellman-Ford is a common alternative when negative weights are allowed. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: Bellman-Ford is a common alternative when negative weights are allowed. The evidence supports the original reply.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

questions.prompt: What property must a directed graph have before a topological ordering can exist?

answers.text: It needs to be directed and acyclic. A directed cycle creates impossible ordering constraints.

feedback.legit: For a directed acyclic graph. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: For a directed acyclic graph. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: Every directed graph has one. Cycles simply appear as consecutive vertices in the result.

feedback.legit: A directed cycle makes a topological ordering impossible. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: A directed cycle makes a topological ordering impossible. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: The ordering is not necessarily unique. Independent vertices can often trade positions without breaking any edge constraint.

feedback.legit: A graph can have more than one valid topological ordering. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: A graph can have more than one valid topological ordering. The evidence supports the original reply.

reference.title: NIST Dictionary of Algorithms and Data Structures

questions.prompt: Red-black trees call themselves balanced, but how strong is that balance and what complexity does it buy?

answers.text: Every subtree has exactly the same shape and height. Red-black invariants enforce perfect balance.

feedback.legit: Red-black trees allow controlled imbalance. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: Red-black trees allow controlled imbalance. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: All root-to-leaf paths contain exactly the same total number of nodes, regardless of color.

feedback.legit: Red-black invariants equalize black height, not the total node count of every path. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: Red-black invariants equalize black height, not the total node count of every path. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: Lookup is constant time in the worst case because rotations keep the root close to every key.

feedback.legit: The height is logarithmic, so lookup is O(log n) in the worst case. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: The height is logarithmic, so lookup is O(log n) in the worst case. Your vote matches the correction.
